en.iper_p1: They can be generalized (e.g., Addison's disease, hemochromatosis, vitamin deficiencies) or localized.
en.ipe_ef_l1: Characteristics: Yellow-brown macules, flat, with blurred margins, typical of fair skin phototypes (I-II).
en.ipe_ef_l2: Etiology: Induced by UV rays, causing increased tyrosinase activity and melanosome size, without an increase in melanocyte number.
en.ipe_ef_l3: Course: Worsen in summer and regress in winter. They are not neoplastic lesions.
en.ipe_lent_l1: Characteristics: Flat macules (not raised), hyperpigmented, with sharp margins ('cookie-cutter'). Unlike ephelides, they persist year-round and are linked to photoaging.
en.ipe_lent_l2: Histopathology: Linear hyperplasia of melanocytes along the dermo-epidermal junction.
en.ipe_lent_l3: Treatment: Cryotherapy, Laser (Q-switched), or topical depigmenting agents.
en.ipe_nevi_l1: Characteristics: Benign proliferation of melanocytes ('nevus cells'). They can be junctional (flat), compound (slightly raised), or dermal (tuberous/papillomatous).
en.ipe_nevi_l2: Oncological Risk: Dysplastic nevi or atypical junctional nevi carry a higher risk of transformation into malignant melanoma.
en.ipe_nevi_w: Monitor: Asymmetry, irregular Borders, variable Color, Diameter >6mm, Evolution (rapid changes). Additional signs: bleeding, itching, erythematous halo (Meyerson's sign).
en.ipe_altre_l: Melasma (Chloasma gravidarum), Solar Lentigo, Post-inflammatory Hyperpigmentation, Tinea Versicolor (hyperchromic type).
en.ipo_p1: Conditions characterized by a reduction or absence of melanin.
en.vit_l1: Etiology: Chronic autoimmune pathology: selective destruction of melanocytes mediated by cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CD8+), with strong genetic predisposition.
en.vit_l2: Clinical Features: Acromic macules (milky white) with sharp margins, often symmetrical. Leukotrichia (white hair in the affected area) is frequent and indicates a reserved prognosis.
en.vit_l3: Management: Depigmented skin lacks natural UV protection (risk of sunburn and carcinomas). Rigorous photoprotection (SPF 50+) is required.
en.alb_l1: Etiology: Group of recessive genetic disorders characterized by absence or reduction of melanin synthesis. Melanocytes are present but functionally defective (enzymatic deficit, e.g., tyrosinase).
en.alb_l2: Clinical Features: White skin, white or straw-blonde hair, gray-blue or translucent irises (red reflex).
en.alb_l3: Ocular Complications: Foveal hypoplasia (underdevelopment of the fovea), nystagmus (involuntary eye movements), severe photophobia, strabismus, and low vision due to anomalous decussation of optic fibers.
en.ipo_altre_l: Pityriasis Alba (atopic dermatitis), Tinea Versicolor (hypochromic type), Nevus Anemicus, Idiopathic Guttate Hypomelanosis.
en.les_tit: PRIMARY AND SECONDARY SKIN LESIONS: Classification and Diagnosis 🔬
en.les_p1: Primary lesions constitute the fundamental morphological unit of the cutaneous response to endogenous or exogenous stimuli. Dermatological diagnosis relies on the accurate analysis of their morphology, topographic distribution, configuration, and arrangement.
en.les_prim_d: Represent the initial, unmodified manifestation of skin pathology. They appear on seemingly healthy skin (e.g., macule, papule, nodule, vesicle, bulla, pustule, wheal).
en.les_sec_d: Derive from the spontaneous evolution of a primary lesion or are induced by external factors (trauma, scratching, therapeutic treatments). Examples include: crust, scale, erosion, ulceration, fissure, scar, lichenification.
en.les_ps_d: Pathological conditions where primary lesions (active phase) and secondary lesions (evolutionary or regressive phase) coexist simultaneously. A classic example is eczema, where erythema and vesicles (primary) are associated with excoriations, erosions, and crusts (secondary).
en.eritema_d1: Vascular skin discoloration caused by vasodilation of the capillaries in the papillary dermis. Color ranges from bright red to purplish. Characterized by positive diascopy (blanches under vitropressure).
en.eritema_d2: Can be transient (physiological: heat, emotion) or persistent (pathological: rosacea, lupus). A hallmark sign of inflammation, infections, hypersensitivity reactions, or photodamage.
en.eritema_d3: Clinical presentations: Infectious exanthems, sunburn, rosacea, erythema multiforme. Morphologies: scarlatiniform, roseoliform, morbilliform.
en.mac_d1: Circumscribed change in skin color, flat surface (non-palpable). Characterized by negative diascopy (does not blanch under pressure), indicating a pigmentary or hemorrhagic alteration.
en.mac_l1: Hyperchromic: Increase in melanin (e.g., freckles, solar lentigo, melasma).
en.mac_l2: Hypochromic/Achromic: Reduction or absence of melanin (e.g., vitiligo, pityriasis versicolor).
en.mac_l4: Pseudomacule: Deposition of exogenous pigments (tattoos) or endogenous pigments (hemosiderin, carotenoderma).
en.mac_d2: Size criteria: Macule (≤ 1 cm); Patch (> 1 cm or due to confluence).
en.pap_d1: Papule: Solid, circumscribed elevated lesion, ≤ 1 cm. Originates from epidermal proliferation (acanthosis), material accumulation (amyloidosis), or inflammatory infiltrate in the papillary dermis. Generally resolves without scarring.
en.pap_d2: Plaque: Solid, elevated lesion, > 1 cm, often resulting from the confluence of papules or centrifugal expansion of an infiltrative process.
en.pap_d3: Associated conditions: Molluscum contagiosum, lichen planus, psoriasis (plaques), subacute eczema.
en.nod_d1: Nodule: Solid, circumscribed elevated lesion, > 1 cm. Involves the reticular dermis or superficial hypodermis. Proliferative (neoplasms) or granulomatous inflammatory origin. May result in scarring.
en.nod_d2: Nodosity: Solid, deep lesion extending to the deep dermis and hypodermis. Palpable as an infiltrative mass, often painful and erythematous. Typical of erythema nodosum or panniculitis.
en.pomfo_d1: Circumscribed, edematous, and transient skin elevation. Onsets rapidly and regresses spontaneously within 24 hours without leaving sequelae. Pathogenesis: papillary dermis edema from vasodilation and increased capillary permeability (histamine).
en.pomfo_d2: Morphology: variable shape (round, geographic), red or pearly-white color (edema compressing the vessels), surrounded by an erythematous flare. Key symptom: intense pruritus.
en.pomfo_d3: Diagnosis: Urticaria (acute/chronic), insect bites, superficial angioedema.
en.vesc_d1: Circumscribed collection of free fluid (serous, serosanguinous) forming a cavity (splitting) within the epidermis or at the dermo-epidermal junction.
en.vesc_d2: Vesicle: Diameter ≤ 5 mm. Intraepidermal (e.g., eczema, herpes) or junctional splitting (e.g., initial varicella). Evolution: rupture (erosion) or reabsorption.
en.vesc_d3: Bulla: Diameter > 5 mm. Clear or turbid fluid. Splitting is often subepidermal or deep junctional. Associated with burns, bullous pemphigoid, dermatitis herpetiformis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
en.vesc_d4: Note: The term Phlyctena is a clinical synonym for bulla, often used for burns or contact blisters.
en.pustola_d1: Circumscribed collection of purulent exudate (neutrophils, cellular debris, bacteria, or sterile) located in the epidermis or hair follicle. Appearance: yellow-white elevation on an erythematous base.
en.pustola_d2: Variable size (from pinpoint to >1 cm). Evolution: rupture (honey-colored crust) or reabsorption. Can be primary or secondary to the transformation of vesicles.
en.pustola_d3: Etiology: Bacterial infections (impetigo, folliculitis), acne vulgaris, pustular psoriasis (sterile), candidiasis.
en.pustola_d4: Sterile pustule: Collection of neutrophils in the absence of live infectious agents (e.g., Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis - AGEP).
en.crosta_d1: Solid mass adhering to the skin surface, consisting of dried exudate (serum, blood, pus), cellular debris, and microorganisms. Covers erosive or ulcerative lesions.
en.crosta_d2: Color classification: Hemorrhagic (brownish-red/black); Serous (yellow honey-colored); Purulent (yellowish-green).
en.crosta_d3: Variants: A crust-scale forms when exudate mixes with desquamating stratum corneum lamellae. Typical of impetigo, infected eczema, herpes zoster.
en.squama_d1: Visible accumulation of stratum corneum lamellae, due to an alteration in the keratinization process (hyperkeratosis) or an accelerated epidermal turnover (parakeratosis).
en.squama_d2: Morphology: Pityriasiform (fine, like dandruff), lamellar (thin sheet), ostraceous (thick and stratified), foliaceous (broad scales). Typical of psoriasis, ichthyosis, seborrheic dermatitis, pityriasis versicolor.
en.escor_d1: Loss of substance limited to the epidermal layer (or superficial papillary dermis), of mechanical/traumatic origin (e.g., scratching, abrasion). Presents an erythematous and sometimes exudative base. Heals without scarring (repair via regeneration).
en.eros_d1: Loss of substance affecting the entire epidermis down to the papillary dermis, without destructively crossing the basement membrane. Results from the spontaneous rupture of vesicles, bullae, or pustules. Moist, erythematous base. Heals without scarring.
en.ulc_d1: Deep loss of substance affecting the entire epidermis and dermis (down to the reticular dermis or hypodermis). Results from tissue necrosis, ischemia, deep infectious processes, or neoplasms. The base may present granulation or necrotic tissue. Always heals with scarring.
en.ragade_d1: Linear and transverse cleavage through the skin layers perpendicular to skin tension lines, involving the epidermis and potentially extending into the dermis. Caused by loss of skin elasticity in movement sites (folds, joints, lips, anus). Frequent pain. If deep, heals with a linear scar.
en.cicat_d1: Reparative outcome of lesions that damaged the dermis. Consists of fibrous connective tissue (collagen) replacing normal tissue. Characterized by the absence of skin appendages (hair follicles, sweat/sebaceous glands) and an altered pigmentation pattern (hypo- or hyperchromia). Can be atrophic, hypertrophic, or keloidal.
en.fust_p1: Hair shaft abnormalities are structural alterations that compromise the mechanical integrity of the fiber, classifiable by the level of damage (cuticle, cortex, medulla) and etiology (traumatic vs. genetic). These conditions are frequently associated with forms of fragility-induced alopecia.
en.tri_nod_mor_d: Focal loss of the cuticle resulting in exposure and fraying of cortical fibers. Nodules form which, upon transverse fracture, assume the characteristic "brush-like" appearance (brush-like fracture), resembling two brooms pushed together.
en.tri_nod_ezi_d: Predominantly acquired from cumulative physical-chemical trauma (thermal, mechanical, chemical). Rarely congenital (e.g., argininosuccinic aciduria, mitochondrial syndromes).
en.tri_sch_mor_d: Clean and sharp transverse fracture of the shaft, without cortical fraying. The hair often appears flattened and rough. Under polarized light, it shows the typical "tiger tail" pattern.
en.tri_sch_ezi_d: Congenital defect of keratinization associated with sulfur deficiency and cystine-rich proteins (< 50% of normal).
en.tri_sch_ass_d: Distinctive sign of Trichothiodystrophy (often associated with ichthyosis, developmental delay, and photosensitivity).
en.tri_inv_mor_d: Intussusception deformity: the rigid distal part of the hair penetrates into the softer, not fully keratinized proximal part, creating a "ball-and-socket" configuration. Upon fracture, the proximal end appears as a "golf-tee hair".
en.tri_inv_ass_d: Pathognomonic sign of Netherton Syndrome (SPINK5 gene mutation), associated with ichthyosis linearis circumflexa and atopic diathesis.
en.fra_fus_mor_d: Fracture that occurs at progressive narrowings of the shaft. The remaining fragment presents a tapered end, similar to a "pencil point".
en.fra_fus_ass_d: Activity marker in alopecia areata ("exclamation mark" hairs or tapered hairs) and in dystrophic anagen, indicating an abrupt arrest of shaft growth.
en.tri_ptil_mor_d: Distal or intermediate longitudinal splitting of the shaft ("split ends"). It represents the final stage of cuticle damage (weathering) that exposes the cortex to fibrillar separation.
en.tri_ptil_ezi_d: Exclusively of acquired origin, caused by repeated mechanical stress and chemical-physical degradation of the hair end.
en.tri_clas_mor_d: Incomplete transverse fracture affecting the medulla and cortex, but with sparing of the cuticle (which remains intact as a "bridge" over the fracture point). Also known as a "greenstick fracture".
en.tri_clas_ezi_d: Consequence of mild physical-chemical trauma or moderate traction that fails to completely break the outer fiber.
en.alt_str_intro: These abnormalities affect the longitudinal or transverse morphology of the hair, arising from keratinization defects in the follicular bulb. The key distinction lies in the regularity of the defect (often genetic) versus irregularity (often acquired/traumatic).
en.moni_mor_d: Shaft with periodic variation in diameter: elliptic nodes (thick, resembling normal hair) alternating with tapered internodes (thin, lacking a medulla). The cuticle is often intact on the nodes but defective on the internodes, where fracture occurs. On trichoscopy, the nodes appear regular and uniformly spaced.
en.moni_ezi_d: Genetic defect of keratinization. Autosomal Dominant form (mutations in KRT81, KRT83, KRT86 genes) or Autosomal Recessive form (mutations in DSG4 gene, encoding Desmoglein 4). Often associated with keratosis pilaris.
en.pseudo_moni_desc: Considered an artifact rather than a genetic condition. It is distinguished by:
en.pseudo_moni_l3: Acquired origin (mechanical trauma from brushing or compression), non-hereditary.
en.pseudo_moni_l4: Fractures, if present, occur predominantly at the nodes (stress points).
en.pili_torti_mor_d: Flattened shaft with axial twists of 90°–180° (up to 360°) at irregular intervals (0.5–1 mm). The cross-section becomes elliptical at the twisted points. Extremely fragile and short (length often < 5 cm). Under incident light, they show a sparkling reflection.
en.pili_torti_ezi_d: Can be isolated (mutations in BCS1L gene in Björnstad Syndrome) or syndromic. Classical form associated with Menkes Syndrome (mutation in ATP7A gene, copper deficiency, X-linked recessive inheritance). Can be acquired from trauma or inflammation.
en.pili_torti_diag_d: Under polarized light, the twists create alternating light and dark bands. A cornerstone sign for screening copper metabolism defects.
en.cap_aff_mor_d: Presence of progressive narrowing along the shaft ending in a thin point. The cuticle remains intact. When the narrowing is very abrupt and distal, they are defined as "exclamation mark hairs".
en.cap_aff_ezi_d: Indicates a sudden slowing or temporary arrest of the mitotic activity of the follicular matrix. Activity marker in alopecia areata, but also present in trichotillomania, severe systemic diseases, or cytotoxic therapies.
en.scanalature_mor_d: Longitudinal grooves running along the shaft, caused by zonal defects in cortex formation. The cuticle remains intact over the groove. In cross-section, the hair appears triangular or "heart-shaped".
en.scanalature_ezi_d: Isolated presence is common and non-pathological. A high percentage (>50%) is strongly associated with Uncombable Hair Syndrome (UHS), Trichothiodystrophy, or ectodermal dysplasias.
en.pili_annulati_morfologia:
Shaft with alternating light and dark bands ("zebra-like appearance").
Reflected light: the bands appear light (shiny).
Transmitted light: the same bands appear dark.
Caused by air-filled microcavities in the cortex due to cellular misalignment.
en.pili_annulati_eziologia: Generally hereditary (autosomal dominant, locus 12q), but can be sporadic. Unlike other abnormalities, the hair is not fragile; the alteration is predominantly cosmetic.
en.pili_bifurcati_morfologia: The shaft splits into two distinct branches, each with its own cuticle, before eventually rejoining more distally.
en.pili_bifurcati_eziologia: Hypothesized formation of a temporary secondary matrix or partial duplication of the dermal papilla during anagen. Differs from trichoptilosis, which is an acquired splitting of a single shaft.
en.pili_multigemini_morfologia: Rare abnormality with 2–10 hair shafts emerging from a single follicular ostium. Each shaft has its own cuticle and inner root sheath, but they share a common outer root sheath.
en.pili_multigemini_caratteristiche: Distinguished from Trichostasis Spinulosa (aggregates of telogen vellus hairs trapped in a follicle dilated by a keratin plug) and from Compound Follicle (multiple independent follicles converging). In Pili Multigemini, all shafts are usually in a structured anagen phase.
en.anomalie_residue_titolo: RESIDUAL ABNORMALITIES AND HAIR SHAFT CURVATURE ALTERATIONS ➰
en.anomalie_residue_intro: This section groups defects acquired from mechanical trauma (knotting) and congenital abnormalities that alter the cross-sectional geometry or curvature of the shaft, affecting its combability and cosmetic appearance without necessarily inducing fracture-related fragility.
en.triconodosi_def_d: An exclusively acquired abnormality characterized by the formation of true knots along the hair shaft (single-strand knots). At the knot level, the cuticle is lifted, fractured, or absent, exposing the cortex.
en.triconodosi_pat_d: Caused by repeated mechanical trauma (aggressive brushing, friction, tight hairstyles). The ribbon-like or flattened shape of curly hair favors tangling and knot formation ("spring effect").
en.triconodosi_freq_d: Prevalent in individuals with curly, coily, or very fine hair (African ethnic hair type or curly Caucasian hair). Rarely spontaneous in extremely long and unkempt hair.
en.capelli_impetinabili_intro: Note: The term "Pili Canaliculi" refers specifically to the microscopic sign (grooves), while the clinical syndrome is UHS. A pediatric condition that tends to improve spontaneously during adolescence.
en.capelli_impetinabili_morf_d: The hair strands present a triangular or "heart-shaped" cross-section (rather than cylindrical) with deep longitudinal grooves (pili canaliculi). This geometry prevents the hair from reflecting light uniformly (giving a silvery/straw-blonde appearance) and from adhering to one another.
en.capelli_impetinabili_ezi_d: Caused by mutations in key genes for hair matrix formation: PADI3 (modifies trichohyalin), TGM3 (stabilizes proteins), TCHH (structural backbone). Inheritance is predominantly autosomal recessive.
en.capelli_impetinabili_fisio_d: Premature or defective keratinization of the inner root sheath, which deforms the growing shaft before it is fully hardened.
en.capelli_lanosi_morf_d: Shafts with an ovoid or flattened cross-section, characterized by tight axial twists and irregular curvatures that give a "woolly" or frizzy appearance. Unlike Pili Torti, the twists are less regular and the hair is generally less fragile.
en.capelli_lanosi_class_d:
Hereditary (Generalized): Involves the entire scalp. Autosomal Dominant or Recessive (often associated with cardio-cutaneous syndromes such as Naxos or Carvajal due to mutations in desmosomal genes).
Localized (Woolly Hair Nevus): A circumscribed patch of woolly hair on an otherwise normal scalp. Non-hereditary, often associated with epidermal nevi.
Acquired: Rare, associated with medications (e.g., retinoids) or androgenetic alopecia.
en.capelli_lanosi_fisio_d: Reduced duration of the anagen phase, which limits the maximum length of the hair to a few centimeters. The hair appears "uncombable" due to the density of the curls, not due to breakage.
en.seborrea_intro: Seborrhea is defined as an increased production of sebum (seborrhea oleosa) or a qualitative alteration of its lipid composition (seborrhea sicca). It is not a disease in itself, but a physiological dysfunction that alters the hydrolipid film, creating a favorable substrate for pathological conditions such as acne, dandruff, and seborrheic dermatitis.
en.seborrea_fisiologica_d: Normal activity of the holocrine glands necessary to form the hydro-lipid mantle (barrier protection) and lubricate the hair shaft. It varies based on age, sex, and genetics.
en.seborrea_patologica_d: Quantitative overproduction of sebum that makes the skin and hair greasy. It can lead to sebaceous retention (oxidized sebum stagnating in the follicles), comedone formation, and alteration of the skin microbiota.
en.seborrea_secca_d: Qualitative alteration of sebum: deficiency of essential lipids (e.g., linoleic acid) and increase in oxidized squalene. The surface appears dry and flaky despite glandular activity, often associated with a compromised skin barrier.
en.cause_ormonali_d: The main regulator is 5-alpha-dihydrotestosterone (DHT), derived from testosterone via the 5-alpha-reductase type I enzyme present in the sebocyte. DHT stimulates sebocyte proliferation and lipid synthesis (via SREBP activation). IGF-1 and insulin can also enhance this effect.
en.cause_composizione_d: In "acneic" or pathological sebum, a reduction in linoleic acid (essential for follicular cohesion) and an increase in squalene and waxes are often observed. Squalene is highly susceptible to oxidation, generating pro-inflammatory free radicals.
en.fattori_microbici_d: The lipophilic yeast Malassezia (specifically globosa and restricta species) proliferates in excess sebum. Its lipase enzymes hydrolyze sebaceous triglycerides, releasing free fatty acids (e.g., oleic acid) which, by penetrating the stratum corneum, induce irritation, inflammation, and hyperproliferation of keratinocytes (dandruff/dermatitis).
en.seborrea_alopecia: Seborrheic / Androgenetic Alopecia: Seborrhea is a risk co-factor. Perifollicular inflammation induced by oxidized sebum and Malassezia can accelerate follicle miniaturization in genetically predisposed individuals.
en.seborrea_follicolite: Folliculitis and Acne: Follicular obstruction from hyperkeratosis and dense sebum favors the proliferation of Cutibacterium acnes and pustular inflammation.
en.seborrea_pitiriasi: Pityriasis Steatoides: An intermediate form between dry dandruff and seborrheic dermatitis. Characterized by greasy, yellowish, and adherent scales soaked in oxidized sebum.
en.seborrea_dermatite: Seborrheic Dermatitis: A chronic inflammatory condition (erythema + desquamation) in sebaceous areas (scalp, eyebrows, sides of the nose). Distinguished from simple seborrhea by the presence of active inflammation and itching.
en.seborrea_terapia_intro: Treatment aims to reduce microbial load, normalize keratinocyte turnover, and regulate sebaceous secretion without compromising the skin barrier (avoiding the rebound effect from harsh cleansing).
en.terapia_antimicrobica_d:
Ketoconazole / Ciclopirox Olamine: Drastically reduce the Malassezia population.
Zinc Pyrithione / Piroctone Olamine: Antifungal and antibacterial action, with a mild cytostatic effect on keratinocyte proliferation.
en.terapia_cheratolitica_d:
Salicylic Acid: Promotes flakes exfoliation and penetrates the follicle.
Sulfur / Clays: Sebum-absorbing and mildly antiseptic action.
en.terapia_sebo_regolatrice_d:
Topical/Systemic Antiandrogens: (e.g., Serenoa Repens, Spironolactone) act on the DHT pathway.
Topical Corticosteroids: Used briefly to reduce acute inflammation in seborrheic dermatitis.
Niacinamide / Green Tea: Antioxidants that reduce lipid peroxidation and inflammation.
en.seborrea_fisioterapia: The combination with physical therapies such as High Frequency (disinfecting, ozonizing, and sebostatic action) or Laser Therapy (biostimulation and anti-inflammatory) can enhance the efficacy of topical treatments.
en.forfora_intro: Dandruff is a chronic non-inflammatory (or mildly inflammatory) condition characterized by visible flaking of the scalp. It is caused by an accelerated epidermal turnover: the renewal time of keratinocytes is reduced from approximately 28 days to 2–7 days. The cells do not reach full maturity and clump into visible scales before shedding.
en.forfora_fattori_titolo: Pathophysiology: The Role of the Microbiota and Barrier
en.forfora_malassezia_d: Dandruff requires the presence of the lipophilic yeast Malassezia (specifically M. globosa and M. restricta species). This fungus possesses lipase enzymes that hydrolyze sebum triglycerides, releasing free fatty acids (e.g., oleic acid). In susceptible individuals, oleic acid penetrates the stratum corneum, inducing irritation, sub-clinical inflammation, and hyperproliferation of keratinocytes (parakeratosis).
en.forfora_barriera_d: The dandruff-affected stratum corneum displays a compromised barrier function and increased permeability (elevated TEWL). This facilitates the penetration of irritants and water loss, creating a vicious cycle of dryness and flaking.
en.forfora_suscettibilità_d: Not all Malassezia carriers develop dandruff. The condition depends on a triad: excess sebum (substrate), presence of the fungus, and individual susceptibility (genetics, immune status, barrier integrity).
en.forfora_secca_d: Scales are fine, white or grayish, loose, and poorly adherent. The underlying skin is generally normal or slightly xerotic. Itching is mild or absent. Typical of the winter season (dry air).
en.forfora_grassa_d: Scales are large, greasy, and yellowish, often clustered into adherent plaques. The underlying skin is erythematous, oily, and inflamed. It is considered a mild form of seborrheic dermatitis and requires a more aggressive treatment.
en.forfora_terapia_intro: The therapeutic goal is to control the Malassezia population, normalize keratinocyte turnover, and reduce inflammation, while simultaneously restoring the skin barrier.
en.forfora_antimicotici_d:
Ketoconazole (2%): A broad-spectrum azole that inhibits ergosterol synthesis in the fungal membrane.
Zinc Pyrithione (ZPT): A multifunctional agent with antifungal, antibacterial activity and a mild cytostatic effect (reduces keratinocyte proliferation).
Ciclopirox Olamine / Selenium Sulfide: Effective alternatives for resistant strains or maintenance treatments.
en.forfora_cheratolitici_d:
Salicylic Acid: Promotes chemical exfoliation ("unsticking" the scales) and penetrates the follicle. It lacks direct antifungal activity but enhances the penetration of antifungals.
Sulfur: Keratolytic and sebostatic action.
en.forfora_naturali_d: Tea Tree Oil (Melaleuca alternifolia): Proven antifungal activity against Malassezia species in clinical trials, useful as an adjuvant.
en.forfora_ph_paragrafo: It is essential to use shampoos with an acidic pH (4.0–5.5) to respect the physiological hydrolipid mantle and create an unfavorable environment for fungal proliferation (which prefers neutral/alkaline pH).
en.forfora_tempo_posa_paragrafo: Contact time is crucial: the therapeutic shampoo must sit for 3–5 minutes before rinsing. Studies show that a contact time of less than 2 minutes drastically reduces the antifungal and cytostatic efficacy of the active ingredients.
en.simple_title_main: HAIR: WHAT IT IS MADE OF AND ITS LIFE CYCLE
en.simple_anatomy_p1: Hairs and hair are filaments made of keratin. They grow from a "sac" in the skin called the HAIR FOLLICLE.
en.simple_anatomy_p2: The follicle is located under the scalp skin (dermis). Its final, concave part is called the BULB.
en.simple_figure_anatomy_caption: Anatomical section of the PILOSEBACEOUS UNIT.
en.simple_anatomy_p3: Hair length, thickness, and location change based on where they are, sex, age, and race.
en.simple_part_fusto: The part that exits the skin is called the SHAFT (or STEM).
en.simple_part_radice: The part under the skin (inside the follicle) is called the ROOT.
en.simple_part_papilla: At the bottom of the root is the DERMAL PAPILLA. This papilla brings nourishment to the hair.
en.simple_part_matrice: Between the bulb and the root is the HAIR MATRIX. The matrix creates all the hair components.
en.simple_part_unit: These parts form the PILOSEBACEOUS UNIT.
en.simple_follicle_text1: The follicle is an inclined "sac" in the skin, which descends 1-3 mm into the dermis. It contains the hair and its sheaths.
en.simple_follicle_text2: Each person has about 5 million follicles. About 100,000 are on the scalp. This number is established before birth and does not increase further.
en.simple_gland_text1: This gland produces sebum, a fatty substance (triglycerides, cholesterol, etc.).
en.simple_gland_text2: Sebum serves to lubricate, soften, and protect the hair as it grows. Sebum spreads onto the skin and hair. Its activity is controlled by hormonal and dietary factors.
en.simple_muscle_text1: It is made of smooth muscle fibers. It attaches to the follicle.
en.simple_muscle_text2: When it contracts (due to cold or emotions like fear), the hair stands up straight. This causes goosebumps. It also helps sebum to exit.
en.simple_structure_p1: Hair is a complex fiber. It contains water (10-30%) and fats (sebum, waxes, etc.) for protection.
en.simple_structure_p2: The hair shaft has three layers:
en.simple_layer_cuticola: CUTICLE: It is the outermost part. It is made of flat, overlapping cells, like roof tiles.
en.simple_layer_corteccia: CORTEX: It is located under the cuticle. It is the largest part of the hair. It gives the hair its strength and contains melanin (color).
en.simple_layer_midollo: MEDULLA: It is the central part, almost absent in the shaft.
en.simple_structure_p3: The main molecules are: keratin (structure), melanin (color), and minerals.
en.simple_keratin_p1: Hair is mainly made of keratin. There are two types:
en.simple_keratin_filamentosa: Filamentous Keratin: Forms bundles (fibrils) in the cortex.
en.simple_keratin_amorfa: Amorphous Keratin: Fills the spaces between the bundles, providing stability.
en.simple_stability_h2: HOW DOES THE STRUCTURE REMAIN STABLE?
en.simple_stability_p1: The keratin chains are joined by "bonds" that hold them together. There are 3 types of bonds:
en.simple_bridge_hydrogen_text: These are very weak bonds. They break easily with water or heat. They give the hair flexibility.
en.simple_bridge_saline_text: These are moderately strong bonds. They break with high heat or with acidic/basic substances. They give the hair strength.
en.simple_bridge_disulfide_text1: These are very strong bonds (covalent bonds), made of sulfur atoms.
en.simple_bridge_disulfide_text2: These bonds provide structure (e.g., natural curly hair). They can only be broken with high energy (e.g., chemical treatments).
en.simple_bridge_disulfide_text3: All three types of bonds contribute to the hair's strength.
en.simple_minerals_p1: Minerals are essential. If they are missing, hair becomes fragile, dry, and dull.
en.simple_mineral_ferro: IRON: If missing, it causes fragility and excessive shedding.
en.simple_mineral_rame: COPPER: Essential for color (melanin) and for disulfide bonds.
en.simple_lipids_p2: Phospholipids are important because they help maintain the hair's correct hydration.
en.simple_melanin_p1: Melanin is the natural pigment. It is found in the cortex and is produced by melanocytes.
en.simple_melanin_feomelanin: PHEOMELANIN: Yellow/red color (blonde and red hair).
en.simple_melanin_tricochromes: TRICHOCHROMES: Special pigments for intense red color.
en.simple_melanin_p3: Melanin is derived from the amino acid tyrosine. The different percentage of pigments determines the final hair color.
en.simple_cycle_p1: Hair has cycles of growth and rest. When an old hair falls out, the follicle forms a new one.
en.simple_anagen_p1: It is the longest phase. The hair matrix is very active and makes it grow.
en.simple_anagen_growth: Hair grows about 1-1.5 cm per month.
en.simple_catagen_p1: It is very short, lasting 2-4 weeks. The matrix stops working.
en.simple_telogen_p1: It is the resting period, lasting about 3 months.
en.simple_telogen_p2: The hair only falls out when a new hair is growing and pushes the old one out.
en.simple_figure_cycle_caption: The three phases of the hair life cycle.
en.simple_cycle_p3: Hair shedding in humans is "mosaic": each follicle follows its cycle independently. This ensures there are no periods without hair.
en.simple_cycle_note: Note: each follicle can complete up to 30 life cycles.
